test_cases.title: New session identifier is generated upon successful authentication
preconditions: A valid user account exists in the system
The user has an active pre-authentication session (e.g., obtained by visiting the login page)
steps: Capture the session token/identifier present before authentication (pre-auth token)
Submit valid credentials to the authentication endpoint
Capture the session token/identifier returned after successful login (post-auth token)
test_cases.expected_result: The post-authentication session identifier is different from the pre-authentication session identifier.

test_cases.title: Pre-authentication session token is invalidated after successful login
preconditions: A valid user account exists in the system
The user has an active pre-authentication session with a known session token
steps: Record the pre-authentication session token
Authenticate successfully with valid credentials
Send a request to an authenticated or session-dependent endpoint using the recorded pre-authentication session token
test_cases.expected_result: The server rejects the pre-authentication session token, returning an unauthorized or session-invalid response (e.g., HTTP 401 or equivalent), and does not process the request as an authenticated session.

test_cases.title: Session fixation attack is prevented — pre-supplied session token not adopted post-login
preconditions: A valid user account exists in the system
An attacker-controlled session token has been injected into the client's cookie/header before authentication
steps: Set a known, attacker-controlled session token on the client (e.g., via cookie injection)
Authenticate successfully using valid credentials
Attempt to use the attacker-controlled session token to access an authenticated resource
test_cases.expected_result: The attacker-controlled pre-authentication session token is not promoted to an authenticated session; any request using it is rejected as unauthenticated.

test_cases.title: Failed authentication does not invalidate the existing pre-authentication session
preconditions: The user has an active pre-authentication session
Invalid credentials are available for testing
steps: Record the current pre-authentication session token
Submit invalid credentials to the authentication endpoint
Verify the response indicates authentication failure
Send a request using the original pre-authentication session token
test_cases.expected_result: The pre-authentication session token remains valid (i.e., the server still recognizes the session as an unauthenticated session) and was not invalidated by the failed login attempt.

test_cases.title: Post-authentication session token is functional for authenticated requests
preconditions: A valid user account exists in the system
At least one endpoint requires authentication
steps: Authenticate successfully with valid credentials
Capture the newly issued post-authentication session token
Send a request to an authenticated endpoint using the new session token
test_cases.expected_result: The server accepts the new session token and returns an authenticated response (e.g., HTTP 200 with expected authenticated content).

test_cases.title: Pre-authentication session token cannot be replayed after logout and re-login
preconditions: A valid user account exists in the system
The user completes a full login cycle
steps: Record the pre-authentication session token before the first login
Authenticate successfully and record the new post-authentication token
Log out to end the authenticated session
Authenticate again successfully to create a second new session
Attempt to use the original pre-authentication session token to access a protected resource
test_cases.expected_result: The original pre-authentication session token is still rejected and cannot be used to access protected resources.

assumptions: The system maintains some form of session token or identifier before authentication (e.g., for CSRF protection or anonymous sessions), which is accessible and observable for testing.
The system exposes at least one endpoint that requires an authenticated session, usable to verify session validity.
Session tokens are transmitted via cookies or authorization headers and can be captured and replayed by the test client.
A failed authentication attempt does not constitute 'login completes', so pre-auth session invalidation is not triggered.
notes: The requirement does not specify the transport mechanism for the session identifier (cookie, header, response body), which may affect how test steps capture the token — steps are kept generic to avoid assuming implementation details. The requirement does not define behavior when no pre-authentication session exists (e.g., direct API call with credentials); this scenario is not covered. The requirement does not address concurrent sessions or multi-device scenarios. The exact error response format for an invalidated session is not specified, so expected_result references general unauthorized behavior rather than a specific status code or message.
